placement_decision.rationale: Reception evidence answers a different question from the Lincoln-only metaphor corpus. It may test how readers, listeners, editors, and political actors received or transformed Lincoln rhetoric, but it must not change the Stage 1-8 Lincoln annotation universe.

claim_boundary.rhetoric_in_text: Claims about metaphor structure inside Lincoln-authored or Lincoln-primary documents may use the existing corpus, concordance, controlled outputs, and claim audit.
claim_boundary.audience_reception: Claims about how audiences received, repeated, resisted, misunderstood, or transformed Lincoln rhetoric require separate reception records with source URLs or archival citations.
claim_boundary.prohibited_inference: Do not infer audience belief, public opinion, political causality, or reader uptake from Lincoln-only metaphor counts.

metadata_fields.rule: Controlled type such as newspaper, diary, letter, meeting_report, or political_commentary.
Publication, collection, correspondent, diary holder, or meeting title.
ISO date when known; otherwise a documented date range.
Publication place, meeting location, or author location when available.
Union, Confederate, abolitionist, Democratic, Republican, soldier, civilian, foreign, unknown, or other documented position.
Specific Lincoln document, speech, phrase, or event being received; use corpus doc_id when possible.
repeat, quote, paraphrase, endorse, resist, ridicule, reframe, omit, or transform.
Short quotation when rights permit, otherwise a concise summary with page or item anchor.
Resolvable URL, persistent identifier, or full archival citation.
Public domain, collection terms, fair-use excerpt, or access restriction.
Exactly what the source supports; must distinguish reception evidence from Lincoln textual evidence.

source_types.name: Newspaper reception
source_types.included_if: Article, editorial, reprint, or report explicitly refers to a Lincoln text, phrase, speech, policy, or public event.
source_types.excluded_if: Item merely reports Civil War events without an identifiable Lincoln rhetoric anchor.
source_types.evidence_use: Track public circulation, partisan reframing, quotation, ridicule, endorsement, or omission.

source_types.name: Private letters and diaries
source_types.included_if: Writer explicitly responds to Lincoln, a Lincoln speech/document, or a Lincoln-linked policy event.
source_types.excluded_if: Private war commentary has no recoverable connection to Lincoln rhetoric.
source_types.evidence_use: Track individual uptake while preserving class, region, race, gender, and access limits.

source_types.name: Meeting reports and resolutions
source_types.included_if: Meeting record quotes, paraphrases, endorses, resists, or transforms Lincoln rhetoric.
source_types.excluded_if: Meeting record offers generic partisan position with no Lincoln text anchor.
source_types.evidence_use: Track organized public response without treating resolutions as mass public opinion.

source_types.name: Political commentary and compiled wartime narratives
source_types.included_if: Commentary identifies a Lincoln speech, proclamation, letter, or phrase as an object of interpretation.
source_types.excluded_if: Retrospective synthesis lacks a near-contemporary reception context.
source_types.evidence_use: Track elite, editorial, or compiled interpretations as reception artifacts, not as direct audience evidence.

candidate_sources.name: Chronicling America: Historic American Newspapers
candidate_sources.archival_citation: Library of Congress, Chronicling America: Historic American Newspapers.
candidate_sources.rights_note: Collection-level terms and individual newspaper rights must be checked before redistribution.
candidate_sources.inclusion_rationale: Candidate source for newspaper reprints, editorials, reports, and partisan responses to Lincoln speeches and proclamations.
evidence_limits: OCR quality and title coverage vary by state and year.
Newspaper partisanship must be recorded before interpreting reception stance.

candidate_sources.name: Abraham Lincoln Papers at the Library of Congress
candidate_sources.archival_citation: Library of Congress, Abraham Lincoln Papers, Series 1: General Correspondence and Related Documents.
candidate_sources.rights_note: Collection-level rights and item-level restrictions must be checked before quotation or redistribution.
candidate_sources.inclusion_rationale: Candidate source for letters to Lincoln and related documents that show direct response to Lincoln rhetoric or policy language.
evidence_limits: Incoming correspondence is not representative public opinion.
Letters may reflect access to Lincoln rather than broad audience reception.

candidate_sources.name: The Valley of the Shadow
candidate_sources.archival_citation: Valley of the Shadow digital archive, Augusta County, Virginia, and Franklin County, Pennsylvania.
candidate_sources.rights_note: Project and item terms must be checked before quotation or redistribution.
candidate_sources.inclusion_rationale: Candidate source for community-level diaries, letters, and newspapers from one Northern and one Southern county.
evidence_limits: Two-county design is comparative but not nationally representative.
Reception claims must remain tied to the specific community and source type.

candidate_sources.name: American Rebellion: Henry Ward Beecher Public Meeting Reports
candidate_sources.archival_citation: American Rebellion: Report of the Speeches of the Rev. Henry Ward Beecher, Delivered at Public Meetings in Manchester, Glasgow, Edinburgh, Liverpool, and London.
candidate_sources.rights_note: Nineteenth-century publication; digitized copy and platform terms must still be checked before reuse.
candidate_sources.inclusion_rationale: Candidate source for organized public-meeting response to Union war rhetoric in a foreign audience context.
evidence_limits: Beecher meeting reports are not Lincoln reception unless an item explicitly anchors to Lincoln rhetoric or policy.
Foreign public meetings should be analyzed separately from domestic U.S. audience response.

candidate_sources.name: The Rebellion Record: A Diary of American Events
candidate_sources.archival_citation: Frank Moore, ed., The Rebellion Record: A Diary of American Events with Documents, Narratives, Illustrative Incidents, Poetry, etc.
candidate_sources.rights_note: Nineteenth-century publication; digitized copy and platform terms must still be checked before reuse.
candidate_sources.inclusion_rationale: Candidate source for compiled wartime documents, reports, narratives, and public commentary that may preserve near-contemporary responses.
evidence_limits: Compiled and edited source, not unmediated audience response.
Publication timing and editorial selection must be recorded for every item used.

evidence_rules: A reception record must identify a Lincoln text anchor before it can support a reception claim.
A reception source can show circulation, uptake, resistance, or reframing; it cannot by itself establish public opinion magnitude.
Reception evidence must be analyzed separately from Lincoln-authored metaphor instances.
Every quoted or summarized reception example must include a source URL or archival citation.
Candidate collections do not count as evidence until individual items are selected, cited, and rights-checked.

pilot_records.source_title: Page containing "With malice toward none / with charity for all" motto
pilot_records.audience_position: postwar newspaper readership / civic memory context
pilot_records.lincoln_text_anchor: doc_021 Second Inaugural Address; "with malice toward none; with charity for all"
pilot_records.evidence_quote_or_summary: LOC text view identifies the Second Inaugural phrase "with malice toward none / with charity for all" on this newspaper page. The item is selected to test phrase-circulation metadata for postwar civic memory.
pilot_records.source_url_or_archival_citation: Mower County Transcript (Lansing, MN), July 4, 1872, p. 2, Chronicling America: Historic American Newspapers, Library of Congress, https://www.loc.gov/resource/sn85025431/1872-07-04/ed-1/?sp=2&st=text
pilot_records.rights_note: Published in the United States in 1872; treated as public domain in the United States. Pilot records summarize the item and do not redistribute page images or extended OCR text.
pilot_records.claim_allowed: Supports only a phrase-circulation reception claim for selected Second Inaugural language in one newspaper item. It does not establish audience magnitude, reader belief, or Lincoln textual evidence.

pilot_records.source_title: Page containing "with malice toward none and charity" language
pilot_records.lincoln_text_anchor: doc_021 Second Inaugural Address; "with malice toward none; with charity for all"
pilot_records.evidence_quote_or_summary: LOC text view identifies "with malice toward none" and charity language on this Reconstruction-era Southern newspaper page. The item is selected to test whether a regional newspaper occurrence can be represented with a clear reception-only boundary.
pilot_records.source_url_or_archival_citation: New Orleans Republican (New Orleans, LA), June 1, 1876, p. 1, Chronicling America: Historic American Newspapers, Library of Congress, https://www.loc.gov/resource/sn83016555/1876-06-01/ed-1/?st=text
pilot_records.rights_note: Published in the United States in 1876; treated as public domain in the United States. Pilot records summarize the item and do not redistribute page images or extended OCR text.
pilot_records.claim_allowed: Supports only a selected phrase-circulation claim for Second Inaugural language in one Reconstruction-era Southern newspaper item. It does not quantify public opinion or prove reader uptake.

pilot_records.source_title: Image 1 text page containing Gettysburg Address language
pilot_records.audience_position: postwar newspaper readership / sectional memory context
pilot_records.lincoln_text_anchor: doc_017 Gettysburg Address; new birth of freedom and government-of-the-people clauses
pilot_records.evidence_quote_or_summary: LOC text view identifies Gettysburg Address language on the page, including the new-birth and government-of-the-people clauses. The item is selected to test phrase-circulation metadata for a Southern newspaper context.
pilot_records.source_url_or_archival_citation: The Morning News (Savannah, GA), September 16, 1895, p. 1, Chronicling America: Historic American Newspapers, Library of Congress, https://www.loc.gov/resource/sn86063034/1895-09-16/ed-1/?sp=1&st=text
pilot_records.rights_note: Published in the United States in 1895; treated as public domain in the United States. Pilot records summarize the item and do not redistribute page images or extended OCR text.
pilot_records.claim_allowed: Supports only a phrase-circulation reception claim for Gettysburg Address language in a later newspaper item. It cannot prove how readers understood the phrase and does not change corpus-internal Gettysburg claims.
